$schema: One Small Life — modular organism attachment schema
notes: Normalised coordinates (0..1) within the part canvas. Phaser composes a Container of layered Sprites; each Sprite is positioned so its own `pivot` lands on the named attachment point. Albedo layers are greyscale masks recoloured with setTint (multiplicative) or pre-baked palette variants. Text is NEVER baked into assets.

feeding.desc: Leading edge — mouth/feeding organ, mirrors with facing

movement.desc: Trailing edge — flagellum; cilia use the full rim path

rim.desc: Membrane + cilia follow the body outline path, not a point

mirroring.rule: Flip the whole container on X to face left; feeding/sensory anchors mirror automatically. Never mirror an individual asymmetric part on its own — flip at the container level so highlights stay lit from the same side.

colorMask.approach: Two-layer: tintable greyscale `albedo` (mid-grey base so multiplicative setTint reads true) + separate `detail` layer at fixed colour for shading/organelles.
colorMask.note: setTint is multiplicative — author albedo at ~#B8B8B8 so a #4FD4C4 tint lands on-palette, not darkened.

shadow.note: Single shared shadow primitive scaled to body bounds — never per-part shadows.

coherenceRules: One palette per organism; parts pull tint from the shared palette, never their own hardcoded hue.
One pattern only; pattern accent must come from the same palette.
Membrane style must be compatible with body silhouette (no spiked membrane on a smooth-jelly body).
Feeding + movement are mutually constrained pairs (cilia-rim excludes single flagellum).
Max 2 sensory parts; both share the same sensory family look.
All parts authored at the same stroke weight + corner radius language (rounded-organic).
Light always from top-left; no part carries a conflicting highlight.
Silhouette test: organism must read as one shape at 40px, not a pile of parts.

portraitMethod.framing: portraitSize canvas, body centred, scaled to 0.8 of frame, facing right, neutral pattern lighting
portraitMethod.rule: Portraits are COMPOSED from the identical part set at a fixed camera — never separate raster art — so the portrait always matches the in-game organism.
